subject: ECS
course_description: Introduction to fundamental principles of effective oral communication. Topics may include voice, diction, projection, gestures. Practice may include speaking with visuals, debate, oral interpretation, impromptu, discussion of ethics, analysis of effective and poor communication.
course_notes: (Neurodivergent Approaches to Oral Communication--You are probably familiar with the typical expectations of public speaking: make eye contact, speak smoothly, project confidence. However, disabled scholars, orators, and activists encourage us to look more critically at these expectations-not as objective rules, but as conventions shaped by cultural norms and systems of power. In this course, we'll discuss typical approaches to oral communication while also studying the diverse rhetorical approaches of neurodivergent communicators. Through these examples, we'll discuss questions like: How do Autistic listeners communicate their attention when eye contact is painful? How do ADHD writers build accommodations into their writing practices? How can oral histories of schizophrenia re-shape stigmatized narratives of disability? To engage with these questions, you'll read (or listen to!) scholarship about biology, rhetoric, disability, and culture. This continuing communication course is open to any student interested in examining the relationship between communication and justice in a variety of rhetorical situations.)
